text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 01 June 2020 

Dr Priya Nair 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Kingsway Community Hospital, London  

Dear Dr Priya Nair, 

Ms Aisha Rahman, DOB: 22-08-1992, Hospital No: K847203 NHS No. 9083175621  
Flat 12, 4 Brookside Close, London, SE15 3QH  

Diagnosis: 

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy. The patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy, with generalised tonic–clonic seizures occurring from sleep and focal aware auras occasionally progressing to focal impaired awareness events.

Aetiology: 

No definitive structural lesion identified on MRI; family history of seizures in a first-degree relative reported. EEG with mixed features including generalised spike–wave and independent right temporal sharp activity.

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild asthma on inhaled corticosteroid as required. Vitamin D insufficiency previously treated, currently stable.

Management Plan: 

- Continue sodium valproate modified-release 500 mg mane and 1,000 mg nocte.  
- Maintain clobazam 10 mg nocte as adjunct for nocturnal generalised seizures.  
- Rescue plan discussed: buccal midazolam 10 mg for prolonged generalised tonic–clonic seizure (>5 minutes) or seizure clustering, with instructions provided to family.  
- Driving advice reiterated in accordance with DVLA guidance for combined seizure types; patient has self-declared and remains off driving pending seizure control.  
- Lifestyle: reinforce regular sleep, hydration, and avoidance of known precipitants (patient-reported missed meals and sleep deprivation).  
- Review in four months with repeat trough valproate level and LFTs prior to clinic; consider lacosamide add-on if focal impaired awareness events persist.

Clinical Summary: 

Since the last review, Ms Aisha Rahman reports that events tend to cluster every seven to nine days. On those days, they experience a brief focal aware aura described in their own words as a “tight, metallic taste with a rising flutter in the stomach and a momentary pull to the right side,” lasting 10–20 seconds. They have practiced aura recognition strategies previously and now routinely sit down, slow their breathing, and alert a family member when the warning occurs. This has reduced secondary generalisation on several occasions. Over the same interval, there have been two nocturnal generalised tonic–clonic seizures, each under two minutes, with typical post-ictal confusion and myalgia but no injury. Daytime focal impaired awareness episodes are rare and usually follow the described warning if they cannot implement their strategy in time.

No adverse effects from current medication are reported beyond mild morning sedation, which is acceptable to the patient. Weight is stable; mood is good without features of depression or anxiety. There is no new neurological deficit on examination. We discussed trigger recognition and the patient prioritised consistent sleep and regular meals; they have set phone reminders and are keeping a seizure diary correlating auras, full seizures, and potential triggers. Family have been re-educated in the rescue plan and recovery position.

I will continue to support their self-management approach, given the usefulness of early warning recognition in reducing generalisation. No further investigations requested today beyond routine blood monitoring for valproate. I have provided a work letter confirming variable fatigue every seven to nine days corresponding to their seizure pattern and the need for flexible start times after nocturnal events.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: events tend to cluster every seven to nine days

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements. The letter states: 'events tend to cluster every seven to nine days.' These events include brief focal aware auras on those days; daytime focal impaired awareness episodes are rare, and there have been two nocturnal generalised tonic–clonic seizures over the interval (unspecified duration), so cannot form a reliable rate for those. The clearest, most frequent quantified occurrence is clustering every 7 to 9 days. Apply rule to select the highest frequency; clustering frequency is every 7 to 9 days. Normalize to allowed format: convert 'every seven to nine days' to '1 per 7 to 9 day'.
seizure_frequency_number: events tend to cluster every seven to nine days
